title: Matelea pubescens
taxonomy: Matelea pubescens is classified within the plant family Apocynaceae, which includes other vines and shrubs. This family is part of the larger order Gentianales, a group of flowering plants. Matelea pubescens, like other species within the genus Matelea, is native to the Americas, specifically in the tropical regions. The Apocynaceae family is known for its diverse range of species, from medicinal plants to ornamental flowers. Matelea pubescens, along with its fellow species, contributes to the rich biodiversity of the Americas.
uses: Matelea pubescens is a plant species that has been traditionally used in folk medicine, particularly in its native regions, where it has been reported to have various medicinal properties. The plant has been used to treat a range of ailments, including fever, cough, and respiratory issues. Its leaves and stems are said to have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which may contribute to its medicinal effects. However, further research is needed to confirm the safety and efficacy of Matelea pubescens for medicinal use. More studies are necessary to fully understand its potential benefits and risks.

intro: Matelea pubescens, also known as the "hairy milkvine," is a perennial climber of the Apocynaceae family, native to the tropical regions of Central and South America. Traditionally, Matelea pubescens has been used to treat various health issues, including inflammation, fever, and respiratory problems, due to its reported antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial properties. The bioactive constituents of Matelea pubescens include alkaloids, flavonoids, and glycosides, which are believed to contribute to its medicinal effects. Matelea pubescens is commonly used in herbal preparations, such as teas, infusions, and tinctures, although its use should be approached with caution and under the guidance of a healthcare professional due to potential side effects and interactions.
